name: Short Title Advantage
description: Shorter titles (lower word_count) are associated with higher citation counts.
null_hypothesis: Title word count has no association with citation count after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Colon Signal
description: Titles containing a colon (subtitle structure) predict higher citation counts.
null_hypothesis: Colon presence in the title has no association with citation count after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Question Mark Penalty
description: Titles ending in a question mark predict lower citation counts (Betteridge's law applied to legal scholarship).
null_hypothesis: Question mark presence in the title has no association with citation count after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Case Name Citation Boost
description: Titles containing a case name (v. construction) predict higher citation counts, signaling doctrinal utility.
null_hypothesis: Case name presence in the title has no association with citation count after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Legal Density × Journal Type Interaction
description: Higher legal term density predicts higher citations in specialty journals but not in general journals (audience-matching hypothesis).
null_hypothesis: The interaction between legal_term_density and journal_type has no association with citation count after controlling for their main effects and other covariates.

name: Neologism Download Premium
description: Titles containing neologisms predict higher SSRN download counts but not higher citation counts. Novelty attracts reader attention but not doctrinal reliance.
null_hypothesis: Neologism presence in the title has no association with SSRN downloads or citation count after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Rethinking vs. The X of Y Template
description: Titles using the 'Rethinking X' template predict lower citations than titles using the 'The X of Y' template. 'Rethinking' signals incremental critique; 'The X of Y' signals foundational treatment.
null_hypothesis: There is no difference in citation counts between articles using the tmpl_rethinking_x template and those using the tmpl_the_x_of_y template after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Provocative Title Effect
description: Provocative titles predict higher SSRN downloads but lower citation counts. Provocation attracts initial attention but may reduce perceived scholarly reliability.
null_hypothesis: Provocative language in the title has no association with SSRN downloads or citation count after controlling for journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Novelty Curvilinearity
description: Title novelty (TF-IDF uniqueness relative to the corpus) has a curvilinear (inverted-U) relationship with citations. Moderate novelty is optimal; highly unique titles are inaccessible to audiences.
null_hypothesis: The quadratic term for title_novelty_score has no association with citation count after controlling for the linear term, journal rank, author prestige, publication year, article length, and subject area.

name: Title Effect Larger Outside T14
description: The incremental R-squared attributable to title features is larger for articles in lower-ranked journals than for articles in T14 journals. At T14 journals, author and journal prestige dominate; at lower-ranked journals, title quality may matter more.
null_hypothesis: The incremental R-squared for title features does not differ significantly between T14 and non-T14 journal subgroups.
